1: # Session lifecycle
2: 
3: This repository uses a repeatable lifecycle so AI-assisted edits stay inspectable across sessions.
4: 
5: ## Phases
6: 
7: | Phase | Goal | Typical files | Commit? |
8: |-------|------|---------------|---------|
9: | Explore | Make broader edits across several files | README, notes, checklists | No |
10: | Review | Compare diff scope against intent | `review-checklist.md` | No |
11: | Stage | Limit the commit to project content | staged diff only | No |
12: | Commit | Record durable documentation changes | same project files | Yes |
13: | Verify | Confirm branch position and leftovers | `session-notes.txt` | No |
14: 
15: ## File roles
16: 
17: - `README.md` — durable overview and workflow pointers.
18: - `session-notes.txt` — scratch pad for the current session; may describe uncommitted work.
19: - `codex-change.txt` — timeline of session-by-session edits across tools.
20: - `review-checklist.md` — pre-commit and post-commit verification steps.
21: - `session-lifecycle.md` — this file; maps how the other docs fit together.
22: - `portability-test.txt` — lightweight marker for cross-environment sync tests.
23: 
24: ## Explore phase
25: 
26: When asked to make complex changes without committing:
27: 
28: 1. Touch multiple project files so the diff shows coordinated edits.
29: 2. Update `session-notes.txt` with what changed and what should stay out of the commit.
30: 3. Append a timeline block to `codex-change.txt` describing the uncommitted pass.
31: 4. Refresh README pointers so reviewers know where to look.
32: 5. Leave agent metadata (`AGENTS.md`, `CLAUDE.md`, `GEMINI.md`) untracked unless explicitly requested.
33: 
34: ## Commit-ready transition
35: 
36: Move from explore to commit only when the documentation set is internally consistent:
37: 
38: 1. README points to the same workflow files that changed.
39: 2. `review-checklist.md` names the checks needed for the staged scope.
40: 3. `session-notes.txt` separates durable commit content from scratch context.
41: 4. `codex-change.txt` records the date and session type for the change.
42: 5. Any portability marker supports the same workflow story instead of introducing unrelated test noise.
43: 
44: If one file still describes the change as no-commit while another file is being staged, update the stale wording before committing.
45: 
46: ## Row replacement pass
47: 
48: Use this when a later request turns a dirty follow-up into a committed change:
49: 
50: 1. Replace the previous no-commit rows instead of only appending another block.
51: 2. Update README and checklist guidance so reviewers know why the older wording changed.
52: 3. Keep `codex-change.txt` and `session-notes.txt` in the same commit when both describe the transition.
53: 4. Stage only tracked project documentation files; leave generated agent metadata untracked.
54: 
55: ## Review questions
56: 
57: Before staging the next coordinated commit, answer:
58: 
59: - Do all modified files belong in the same logical change?
60: - Does README content match the actual git state (committed vs dirty)?
61: - Are scratch details confined to `session-notes.txt`?
62: - Did any generated session files get staged by mistake?
63: 
64: ## Suggested commit grouping
65: 
66: When moving from dirty worktree to commit, prefer one commit that includes:
67: 
68: - README and lifecycle doc updates
69: - checklist adjustments
70: - session notes and codex timeline entries that describe the same pass
71: - row replacements that convert stale no-commit wording into the committed scope
72: 
73: Keep portability or environment test markers separate if they are unrelated to documentation workflow changes.
74: 
75: ## Verification after commit
76: 
77: After committing, run `git status --short --branch` and check that only intentionally untracked metadata remains. If a generated metadata file appears next to project files, leave it out unless the task explicitly asks to track it.
78:

1: # god-damn-repo
2: 
3: blyat problema
4: 
5: uncommitted change
6: 
7: Latest session edit (2026-06-20): Gemini uncommitted explore pass — coordinated doc updates left dirty for review.
8: 
9: 
10: ## Getting started
11: 
12: This repository is currently being used to exercise small changes, branch history, and AI session tracking.
13: 
14: ### Documentation map
15: 
16: | File | Purpose |
17: |------|---------|
18: | `session-lifecycle.md` | Phases from explore → review → commit → verify |
19: | `review-checklist.md` | Checklist for staging and post-commit checks |
20: | `session-notes.txt` | Scratch pad for the active session |
21: | `codex-change.txt` | Cross-tool session timeline |
22: | `portability-test.txt` | Environment sync marker |
23: 
24: Start with `session-lifecycle.md` when reviewing a dirty worktree that spans several files.
25: 
26: ### Review matrix
27: 
28: | Review area | Primary file | Commit signal |
29: |-------------|--------------|---------------|
30: | Lifecycle phase | `session-lifecycle.md` | Current edits fit explore, review, stage, commit, or verify |
31: | File scope | `review-checklist.md` | Project files are separated from generated agent metadata |
32: | Session history | `codex-change.txt` | Timeline matches the files in the staged diff |
33: | Working notes | `session-notes.txt` | Scratch notes identify what is durable enough to commit |
34: | Environment marker | `portability-test.txt` | Sync notes are included only when they support the same workflow change |
35: | Row replacement | `session-notes.txt` and `codex-change.txt` | Prior dirty-session rows are updated before commit |
36: 
37: ## Repository notes
38: 
39: The current branch contains a compact history intended for testing how changes move through local commits, remote branches, and AI-assisted sessions. Most updates are intentionally small so their authorship and file impact are easy to inspect.
40: 
41: Useful checks while reviewing this repository:
42: 
43: * Run `git status --short --branch` to see the current branch, local commits, and uncommitted files.
44: * Run `git log --oneline --decorate --all -n 20` to compare recent branch activity.
45: * Run `git diff` before committing so local edits stay explicit.
46: 
47: Session metadata files may appear in the working tree when tooling records agent context. Treat those files separately from project content unless a change explicitly asks to include them.
48: 
49: ## Review workflow
50: 
51: Use this repository as a small, explicit sandbox for checking how AI-assisted edits move from dirty worktree to commit. A typical review pass should answer four questions:
52: 
53: * Which files changed, and are any of them generated session metadata?
54: * Which edits are project content versus temporary scratch notes?
55: * Are staged files limited to the intended commit scope?
56: * Does the commit message describe the visible documentation or behavior change?
57: 
58: Suggested local review loop:
59: 
60: 1. Run `git status --short --branch` to identify branch position and dirty files.
61: 2. Run `git diff` to inspect unstaged changes before staging.
62: 3. Stage only the files that belong in the commit.
63: 4. Run `git diff --cached` to review exactly what will be committed.
64: 5. Commit with a message that names the practical change.
65: 
66: See `review-checklist.md` for a compact checklist and `session-lifecycle.md` for how multi-file explore passes should be grouped before commit.
67: 
68: ### When to commit vs stay dirty
69: 
70: | Situation | Action |
71: |-----------|--------|
72: | Single-file typo or note refresh | Commit immediately if requested |
73: | Coordinated edits across README, notes, and checklists | Review full diff, then commit as one logical group |
74: | Agent metadata files appear untracked | Leave untracked unless explicitly requested |
75: | Scratch exploration not ready for history | Stay dirty; record intent in `session-notes.txt` |
76: 
77: For coordinated documentation updates, commit only after the README map, lifecycle doc, checklist, and timeline all describe the same file set. If any one of those files points to a different scope, keep the worktree dirty and resolve the mismatch first.
78: 
79: Before creating a commit, check that:
80: 
81: * The staged diff contains only files intended for the commit.
82: * The commit message describes the visible behavior or documentation change.
83: * Any generated or session-only files are either ignored or intentionally included.
84: 
85: To make it easy for you to get started with GitLab, here's a list of recommended next steps.
86: 
87: Already a pro? Just edit this README.md and make it your own. Want to make it easy? [Use the template at the bottom](#editing-this-readme)!
88: 
89: ## Add your files
90: 
91: Keep changes small and easy to inspect while the repository is being used for session experiments.
92: 
93: * [Create](https://docs.gitlab.com/user/project/repository/web_editor/#create-a-file) or [upload](https://docs.gitlab.com/user/project/repository/web_editor/#upload-a-file) files
94: * [Add files using the command line](https://docs.gitlab.com/topics/git/add_files/#add-files-to-a-git-repository) or push an existing Git repository with the following command:
95: 
96: ```
97: cd existing_repo
98: git remote add origin https://gitlab.com/dolobanko/god-damn-repo.git
99: git branch -M main
100: git push -uf origin main
101: ```
102: 
103: ## Integrate with your tools
104: 
105: * [Set up project integrations](https://gitlab.com/dolobanko/god-damn-repo/-/settings/integrations)
106: 
107: ## Collaborate with your team
108: 
109: * [Invite team members and collaborators](https://docs.gitlab.com/user/project/members/)
110: * [Create a new merge request](https://docs.gitlab.com/user/project/merge_requests/creating_merge_requests/)
111: * [Automatically close issues from merge requests](https://docs.gitlab.com/user/project/issues/managing_issues/#closing-issues-automatically)
112: * [Enable merge request approvals](https://docs.gitlab.com/user/project/merge_requests/approvals/)
113: * [Set auto-merge](https://docs.gitlab.com/user/project/merge_requests/auto_merge/)
114: 
115: ## Test and Deploy
116: 
117: Use the built-in continuous integration in GitLab.
118: 
119: * [Get started with GitLab CI/CD](https://docs.gitlab.com/ci/quick_start/)
120: * [Analyze your code for known vulnerabilities with Static Application Security Testing (SAST)](https://docs.gitlab.com/user/application_security/sast/)
121: * [Deploy to Kubernetes, Amazon EC2, or Amazon ECS using Auto Deploy](https://docs.gitlab.com/topics/autodevops/requirements/)
122: * [Use pull-based deployments for improved Kubernetes management](https://docs.gitlab.com/user/clusters/agent/)
123: * [Set up protected environments](https://docs.gitlab.com/ci/environments/protected_environments/)
124: 
125: ***
126: 
127: # Editing this README
128: 
129: When you're ready to make this README your own, just edit this file and use the handy template below (or feel free to structure it however you want - this is just a starting point!). Thanks to [makeareadme.com](https://www.makeareadme.com/) for this template.
130: 
131: ## Suggestions for a good README
132: 
133: Every project is different, so consider which of these sections apply to yours. The sections used in the template are suggestions for most open source projects. Also keep in mind that while a README can be too long and detailed, too long is better than too short. If you think your README is too long, consider utilizing another form of documentation rather than cutting out information.
134: 
135: ## Name
136: Choose a self-explaining name for your project.
137: 
138: ## Description
139: Let people know what your project can do specifically. Provide context and add a link to any reference visitors might be unfamiliar with. A list of Features or a Background subsection can also be added here. If there are alternatives to your project, this is a good place to list differentiating factors.
140: 
141: ## Badges
142: On some READMEs, you may see small images that convey metadata, such as whether or not all the tests are passing for the project. You can use Shields to add some to your README. Many services also have instructions for adding a badge.
143: 
144: ## Visuals
145: Depending on what you are making, it can be a good idea to include screenshots or even a video (you'll frequently see GIFs rather than actual videos). Tools like ttygif can help, but check out Asciinema for a more sophisticated method.
146: 
147: ## Installation
148: Within a particular ecosystem, there may be a common way of installing things, such as using Yarn, NuGet, or Homebrew. However, consider the possibility that whoever is reading your README is a novice and would like more guidance. Listing specific steps helps remove ambiguity and gets people to using your project as quickly as possible. If it only runs in a specific context like a particular programming language version or operating system or has dependencies that have to be installed manually, also add a Requirements subsection.
149: 
150: ## Usage
151: Use examples liberally, and show the expected output if you can. It's helpful to have inline the smallest example of usage that you can demonstrate, while providing links to more sophisticated examples if they are too long to reasonably include in the README.
152: 
153: ## Support
154: Tell people where they can go to for help. It can be any combination of an issue tracker, a chat room, an email address, etc.
155: 
156: ## Roadmap
157: If you have ideas for releases in the future, it is a good idea to list them in the README.
158: 
159: ## Contributing
160: State if you are open to contributions and what your requirements are for accepting them.
161: 
162: For people who want to make changes to your project, it's helpful to have some documentation on how to get started. Perhaps there is a script that they should run or some environment variables that they need to set. Make these steps explicit. These instructions could also be useful to your future self.
163: 
164: You can also document commands to lint the code or run tests. These steps help to ensure high code quality and reduce the likelihood that the changes inadvertently break something. Having instructions for running tests is especially helpful if it requires external setup, such as starting a Selenium server for testing in a browser.
165: 
166: ## Authors and acknowledgment
167: Show your appreciation to those who have contributed to the project.
168: 
169: ## License
170: For open source projects, say how it is licensed.
171: 
172: ## Project status
173: If you have run out of energy or time for your project, put a note at the top of the README saying that development has slowed down or stopped completely. Someone may choose to fork your project or volunteer to step in as a maintainer or owner, allowing your project to keep going. You can also make an explicit request for maintainers.
174:

4: 
5: ## Worktree scope

8: - Identify modified project files separately from generated agent metadata.
9: - Confirm untracked files are intentional before staging them.
10: 
11: ## Diff review
12: 
13: - Run `git diff` before staging.
14: - Check that README or note updates describe current repository behavior.
15: - Keep temporary scratch details out of durable docs unless they explain the commit.
16: - When replacing prior session rows, verify the old wording no longer contradicts the new commit state.
17: 
18: ## Multi-file coordination
19: 
20: Use this when several project files changed in one explore pass:
21: 
22: - List every modified file and confirm they share one logical purpose.
23: - Read `session-lifecycle.md` to verify the edit matches the intended phase (explore vs commit-ready).
24: - Cross-check README documentation map against files actually touched.
25: - Confirm `session-notes.txt` records what should be committed vs left dirty.
26: - Confirm `codex-change.txt` timeline matches the files in the diff.
27: - If portability or environment markers changed, decide whether they belong in the same commit or a follow-up.
28: 
29: ## Commit-readiness gate
30: 
31: Before staging a coordinated documentation pass:
32: 
33: - Confirm every durable file named in `session-notes.txt` is either modified or intentionally unchanged.
34: - Confirm every modified project file appears in either the README map or the session lifecycle file roles.
35: - Confirm untracked agent metadata files are still excluded from the commit scope.
36: - Confirm the timeline uses exact dates for any session handoff or no-commit marker.
37: - Confirm any earlier no-commit rows have been replaced or explicitly preserved before committing.
38: - Confirm the final commit message describes the workflow change, not the tooling that produced it.
39: 
40: ## Staging
41: 
42: - Stage only the files that belong in the commit.
43: - Run `git diff --cached` after staging.
44: - Confirm no session-only metadata files are staged accidentally.
45: 
46: ## Commit
47: 
48: - Use a message that describes the visible change.
49: - Prefer small documentation commits while this repo is used for history tracking.
50: - Re-run `git status --short --branch` after committing to see what remains dirty.
51: - If committing a previously dirty explore pass, update the current session note from "no commit" to the actual committed scope before staging.
52: 
53: ## Post-commit
54: 
55: - Confirm the branch is ahead of origin only by the intended new commit.
56: - Verify untracked agent metadata files were not included unless explicitly requested.
57: - Leave any follow-up scratch notes in session-notes.txt for the next session.
58: 
59: ## Uncommitted explore pass (no commit)
60: 
61: When the session intentionally ends with a dirty worktree:
62: 
63: - Do not stage or commit unless explicitly requested.
64: - Confirm the user asked for a dirty worktree (e.g. "make changes but not commit") before closing the session.
65: - Update session-notes.txt with the file list and intended future commit scope.
66: - Append a codex-change.txt timeline block naming the explore pass.
67: - Refresh README latest-session line only if durable docs changed.
68:
